attributes.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of episodic flushing, abdominal pain, and diarrhoea. She also reports multiple reddish-brown spots on her trunk and limbs that become raised and itchy in seconds when rubbed. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Multiple reddish-brown macules are present on her trunk and limbs. Gentle stroking of these lesions produces localised whealing and erythema. A recent bone marrow biopsy has confirmed systemic mastocytosis.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with typical features of cutaneous mastocytosis (urticaria pigmentosa with positive Darier's sign) and systemic symptoms (flushing, gastrointestinal symptoms). It is a difficult diagnosis as symptoms of systemic disease are often non-specific. In this case, the diagnosis has been confirmed by bone marrow biopsy, and a lesional biospy may also have be performed. Urticaria pigmentosa is more typical in childhood and when present in adults, is more resistant to treatment.

Combined H1 and H2 antihistamines are the first-line treatment for systemic mastocytosis. H1 antihistamines (such as cetirizine) help control skin symptoms like pruritus and flushing, while H2 antihistamines (such as ranitidine) help manage gastrointestinal symptoms. This combination effectively controls many of the symptoms caused by mast cell mediator release.

Oral prednisolone is not typically used as first-line therapy for mastocytosis. While it may help with acute severe symptoms, long-term corticosteroid use carries significant side effects and is reserved for refractory cases. Topical steroids can be used for pruritic lesions however only beneficial for localised treatment.

Oral sodium cromoglicate can be useful as an add-on therapy, particularly for gastrointestinal symptoms, but it is not the first-line treatment. It works by stabilising mast cells and preventing mediator release.

Interferon alfa is a second-line treatment option for aggressive systemic mastocytosis that has not responded to antihistamines and other conventional therapies. It can help reduce mast cell numbers but has significant side effects.

Aspirin can cause mast cell degranulation with uritcaria pigmentosa thus should be avoided. Codeine, morphine, alcohol, anticholinergics and non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs should also be avoided in severe disease.

In addition to pharmacological treatment, patients should be educated about avoiding known triggers such as physical stimuli (heat, friction), emotional stress, certain medications, and specific foods that can precipitate symptoms. Phototherapy is another treatment for adults. They should also carry an emergency kit containing antihistamines and, if prescribed, an adrenaline auto-injector due to risk of severe allergic reaction from wasp and bee stings.
learningPoints: Cutaneous mastocytosis typically presents with reddish-brown macules that urticate when stroked (Darier's sign).
Avoidance of triggers (heat, friction, certain medications) and antihistamines are a key component of mastocytosis management.
references: Oakley A, Yung A. Maculopapular cutaneousmastocytosis. DermNet NZ. 2014. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/maculopapular-cutaneous-mastocytosis

Pardanani A, Systemic mastocytosis in adults: 2023 update on diagnosis, risk stratification and management. American Journalo f Hematology. 2023: 98 (7). Available at: https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/ajh.26962#
